reviewed.by: argument+orthodoxy gates; derived from library/phil2.html (orthodoxy-CLEAN 2026-07-04). Isaiah-45-centered recut; Kyrios scene routes confidence through the LXX-fact; slowed pacing.

voiceover: Every knee will bow. Paul wrote that about Jesus — but Isaiah wrote it about God. Tucked into a prison letter, Paul quotes a hymn older than his own letter — the Carmen Christi, the Song of Christ, sung within a generation of the cross. It says Christ was in the form of God, then emptied himself by taking a servant's form, obedient even to death. But the loudest line is the last one — and to hear it, you have to turn back seven hundred years, to Isaiah 45, one of the most fiercely monotheistic chapters in the whole Bible. There God says: I am God, and there is no other. And then he swears an oath by himself: to me every knee shall bow, every tongue shall swear. That is worship owed to the one God of Israel — and to no one else. Now watch what Paul does. He takes that oath — God's own exclusive worship — and he writes: at the name of Jesus every knee should bow, and every tongue confess that Jesus Christ is Lord. The name above every name that Jesus is given is Kyrios — the Lord. And in Greek, that is the very word that stood in for God's own name, YHWH. The worship Isaiah reserved for YHWH alone is now given to Jesus — and monotheism is not abandoned, for it is all to the glory of God the Father. This is no demigod in a pantheon. It is Jesus placed inside the very identity of the one God — what Richard Bauckham calls a Christology of divine identity. And it was not a later invention. It was confessed at the headwaters of the faith. Every knee will bow. The only question is whether yours bows now, in worship, or later, in awe.

lines.t: In a prison letter, Paul quotes
a hymn older than the letter —
Christ was “in the form of God,”
But the loudest line is last.
Worship owed to the one God —
In Greek, that is the very word
that stood in for God's own name:
“to the glory of God the Father.”
the identity of the one God.
at the headwaters of the faith.
